description: Optimize slow dashboard query reducing p99 from 4.2s to under 300ms
system_context: Rails analytics dashboard for SaaS metrics. Tech stack: Ruby 3.3, Rails 7.1, PostgreSQL 16, Redis. Main dashboard query at app/queries/metrics_query.rb takes 4.2s at p99 on accounts with 100k+ events. Users complaining. Need to get under 300ms without changing the API contract.
turns: Profiled the query with rack-mini-profiler in dev. The MetricsQuery#call method does 4 separate queries: aggregate events by day, by event_type, by user, by source. Each full table scan on events table.

Explained each query. EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the by-day aggregation shows Seq Scan on events (cost=0..147832 rows=1042083). No index on (account_id, created_at). That's the root issue — we filter by account_id and group by date but no index supports it.

Checked existing indexes on events. \d events in psql shows indexes on id, account_id alone, user_id alone, created_at alone. No composite index. The planner can't use two single-column indexes effectively for this query.

Plan: 1) Add composite index (account_id, created_at, event_type) to support the aggregations. 2) Rewrite 4 queries as single query using FILTER clause to avoid 4 passes. 3) Consider materialized view for accounts with >500k events.

Created db/migrate/20260419_add_composite_index_to_events.rb. CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_events_account_time_type ON events (account_id, created_at, event_type). Added matching index on (account_id, created_at, user_id). Migration is safe for production — uses CONCURRENTLY, doesn't lock.

Ran migration on staging. Index creation took 38 seconds on 10M row events table. EXPLAIN ANALYZE on by-day aggregation now: Index Scan on idx_events_account_time_type, cost=0..1847, actual time 8ms. Down from 1.1s.

Rewrote app/queries/metrics_query.rb. Single query using aggregation with FILTER clauses: COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE event_type='pageview'), COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE event_type='signup'), etc. And GROUPING SETS to get day/type/user/source rollups in one pass. 142 lines (was 203).
Tested rewrite on dev. Same results as before (compared output JSON line by line). Total query time: 89ms. Down from 4.2s. Rack-mini-profiler shows a single DB call instead of 4.

Added spec/queries/metrics_query_spec.rb tests. 14 tests covering: single event type, multiple types, date ranges, empty results, single user, single source, cross-account isolation, timezone handling. All 14 pass.

Considered materialized view for very large accounts. After rewrite, even accounts with 2M events complete in 340ms. Materialized view adds cache invalidation complexity. Decided against — p99 target met without it.

Added cache layer via Rails.cache.fetch with 60s TTL. Key includes account_id, start_date, end_date. Sliding window: on fresh query, writes result. On repeat query within 60s, serves from cache in <5ms. 23 lines added.
Measured on staging with load test (100 req/s for 5 min using k6). p50: 42ms, p95: 180ms, p99: 267ms. Target of <300ms met. Memory usage stable. Redis cache hit rate: 73%.

Ran full test suite: 1847/1847 passing. Deployed to production behind feature flag perf_dashboard_v2. Enabled for 10% of accounts. Monitored for 1 hour — no errors, p99 went from 4.2s to 248ms on the 10% cohort.
Gradually rolled out to 100%. Final p99 production: 231ms. CPU on DB reduced 40% due to index usage and single-query rewrite. Users stopped complaining. Shipped.
